ALVING. Regina, unfortunately, was allowed to stay at home too
        long. I ought to have taken her earlier into my house.

Yes, but isn't she splendid to look at, mother? [He fills his
        glass.]
ALVING. Regina has many serious faults—
Oh, what does that matter?

ALVING. But I am fond of her, nevertheless, and I am responsible
        for her. I wouldn't for all the world have any harm happen to her.
Mother, Regina is my only salvation!
ALVING.  What do you mean by that?
I cannot go on bearing all this anguish of soul alone.
ALVING. Have you not your mother to share it with you?

Yes; that's what I thought; and so I came home to you. But that
        will not do. I see it won't do. I cannot endure my life here.
ALVING. Oswald!

I must live differently, mother. That is why I must leave you. I
        will not have you looking on at it.
ALVING. My unhappy boy! But, Oswald, while you are so ill as this—

If it were only the illness, I should stay with you, mother, you
        may be sure; for you are the best friend I have in the world.
ALVING. Yes, indeed I am, Oswald; am I not?

But it's all the torment, the
        gnawing remorse—and then, the great, killing dread. Oh—that
        awful dread!
ALVING.  Dread? What dread? What do you mean?

Oh, you mustn't ask me any more. I don't know. I can't describe
        it.
ALVING.
What is it you want?

ALVING. I want my boy to be happy—that is what I want. He
        sha'n't go on brooding over things. 
        More champagne—a large bottle.
Mother!
ALVING. Do you think we don't know how to live here at home?

Isn't she splendid to look at? How beautifully she's built! And
        so thoroughly healthy!

ALVING.  Sit down, Oswald; let us talk quietly
        together.

I daresay you don't know, mother, that I owe Regina some
        reparation.
ALVING. You!

For a bit of thoughtlessness, or whatever you like to call it—very
        innocent, at any rate. When I was home last time—
ALVING. Well?

She used often to ask me about Paris, and I used to tell her one
        thing and another. Then I recollect I happened to say to her one day,
        "Shouldn't you like to go there yourself?"
ALVING. Well?

I saw her face flush, and then she said, "Yes, I should like it
        of all things." "Ah, well," I replied, "it might perhaps be managed"—or
        something like that.
ALVING. And then?

Of course I had forgotten all about it; but the day before
        yesterday I happened to ask her whether she was glad I was to stay at
        home so long—
ALVING. Yes?

And then she gave me such a strange look, and asked, "But what's
        to become of my trip to Paris?"
ALVING. Her trip!

And so it came out that she had taken the thing seriously; that
        she had been thinking of me the whole time, and had set to work to learn
        French—
ALVING. So that was why—!

Mother—when I saw that fresh, lovely, splendid girl
        standing there before me—till then I had hardly noticed her—but
        when she stood there as though with open arms ready to receive me—
ALVING. Oswald!
ALVING.  The joy of life? Can there be salvation in that?

I'm sorry to
        have been so long, but I had to go to the cellar. [Places the bottle on
        the table.]
And now bring another glass.

There is Mrs. Alving's glass, Mr.
        Alving.

Yes, but bring one for yourself, Regina. [REGINA starts and
        gives a lightning-like side glance at MRS. ALVING.] Why do you wait?
Is it Mrs. Alving's wish?
ALVING. Bring the glass, Regina.
REGINA goes out into the dining-room.

Have you noticed how she walks?—so
        firmly and lightly!
ALVING. This can never be, Oswald!

It's a settled thing. Can't you see that? It's no use saying
        anything against it.
REGINA enters with an empty glass, which she keeps in her hand.
Sit down, Regina.
REGINA looks inquiringly at MRS. ALVING.

ALVING. Sit down. [REGINA sits on a chair by the dining room door,
        still holding the empty glass in her hand.] Oswald—what were you
        saying about the joy of life?

Ah, the joy of life, mother—that's a thing you don't know
        much about in these parts. I have never felt it here.
ALVING. Not when you are with me?
Not when I'm at home. But you don't understand that.
ALVING. Yes, yes; I think I almost understand it—now.

And then, too, the joy of work! At bottom, it's the same thing.
        But that, too, you know nothing about.
ALVING. Perhaps you are right. Tell me more about it, Oswald.

I only mean that here people are brought up to believe that work
        is a curse and a punishment for sin, and that life is something
        miserable, something it would be best to have done with, the sooner the
        better.

ALVING. "A vale of tears," yes; and we certainly do our best to
        make it one.

But in the great world people won't hear of such things. There,
        nobody really believes such doctrines any longer. There, you feel it a
        positive bliss and ecstasy merely to draw the breath of life. Mother,
        have you noticed that everything I have painted has turned upon the joy
        of life?—always, always upon the joy of life?—light and
        sunshine and glorious air and faces radiant with happiness. That is why
        I'm afraid of remaining at home with you.
ALVING. Afraid? What are you afraid of here, with me?
I'm afraid lest all my instincts should be warped into ugliness.

ALVING.  Do you think that is what would
        happen?

I know it. You may live the same life here as there, and yet it
        won't be the same life.

ALVING. [Who has been listening eagerly, rises, her eyes big with
        thought, and says:] Now I see the sequence of things.
What is it you see?
ALVING. I see it now for the first time. And now I can speak.
Mother, I don't understand you.
Perhaps I ought to go?

ALVING. No. Stay here. Now I can speak. Now, my boy, you shall know
        the whole truth. And then you can choose. Oswald! Regina!
Hush! The Pastor—

There! We have had a most edifying
        time down there.
So have we.

We must stand by Engstrand and his Sailors' Home. Regina must
        go to him and help him—
No thank you, sir.

What—? You here? And
        with a glass in your hand!
Pardon!
Regina is going with me, Mr. Manders.
Going! With you!
Yes; as my wife—if she wishes it.
But, merciful God—!
I can't help it, sir.
Or she'll stay here, if I stay.
Here!
I am thunderstruck at your conduct, Mrs. Alving.

ALVING. They will do neither one thing nor the other; for now I can
        speak out plainly.
You surely will not do that! No, no, no!

ALVING. Yes, I can speak and I will. And no ideals shall suffer
        after all.
Mother—what is it you are hiding from me?
Oh, ma'am, listen! Don't you hear shouts outside.

What's going on? Where does that
        light come from?
The Orphanage is on fire!
ALVING.  On fire!
On fire! Impossible! I've just come from there.
Where's my hat? Oh, never mind it—Father's Orphanage—!
ALVING. My shawl, Regina! The whole place is in a blaze!

Terrible! Mrs. Alving, it is a judgment upon this abode of
        lawlessness.

ALVING. Yes, of course. Come, Regina. [She and REGINA hasten out
        through the hall.]

And we left it uninsured! [He goes
        out the same way.]

scenes.location:
[The room as before. All the doors stand open. The lamp is still burning
        on the table. It is dark out of doors; there is only a faint glow from
        the conflagration in the background to the left.]

entries.text:
MRS. ALVING, with a shawl over her head, stands in the conservatory,
        looking out. REGINA, also with a shawl on, stands a little behind her.
ALVING. The whole thing burnt!—burnt to the ground!
The basement is still burning.

ALVING. How is it Oswald doesn't come home? There's nothing to be
        saved.
Should you like me to take down his hat to him?
ALVING. Has he not even got his hat on?
No; there it hangs.

ALVING. Let it be. He must come up now. I shall go and look for him
        myself.
Is not Mrs. Alving here?
She has just gone down the garden.
This is the most terrible night I ever went through.
Yes; isn't it a dreadful misfortune, sir?
Oh, don't talk about it! I can hardly bear to think of it.
How can it have happened—?

Don't ask me, Miss Engstrand! How should I know? Do you,
        too—? Is it not enough that your father—?
What about him?
Oh, he has driven me distracted—
Your Reverence—
Are you after me here, too?

Yes, strike me dead, but I must—! Oh, Lord! what am I
        saying? But this is a terrible ugly business, your Reverence.
Alas! alas!
What's the matter?

Why, it all came of this here prayer-meeting, you see.
         The bird's limed, my girl.  And to think it should be
        my doing that such a thing should be his Reverence's doing!
But I assure you, Engstrand—

There wasn't another soul except your Reverence as ever laid
        a finger on the candles down there.

So you declare. But I certainly cannot recollect that
        I ever had a candle in my hand.

And I saw as clear as daylight how your Reverence took the
        candle and snuffed it with your fingers, and threw away the snuff among
        the shavings.
And you stood and looked on?
Yes; I saw it as plain as a pike-staff, I did.

It's quite beyond my comprehension. Besides, it has never been
        my habit to snuff candles with my fingers.

And terrible risky it looked, too, that it did! But is there
        such a deal of harm done after all, your Reverence?
Oh, don't ask me!

And your Reverence hadn't insured it,
        neither?

No, no, no; I have told you
        so.

Not insured! And then to go straight away
        down and set light to the whole thing! Lord, Lord, what a misfortune!

Ay, you may well say that,
        Engstrand.

And to think that such a thing should happen to a benevolent
        Institution, that was to have been a blessing both to town and country,
        as the saying goes! The newspapers won't be for handling your Reverence
        very gently, I expect.

No; that is just what I am thinking of. That is almost the
        worst of the whole matter. All the malignant attacks and imputations—!
        Oh, it makes me shudder to think of it!

ALVING.  He is not to be persuaded to
        leave the fire.
Ah, there you are, Mrs. Alving.
ALVING. So you have escaped your Inaugural Address, Pastor Manders.
Oh, I should so gladly—

ALVING.  It is all for the best. That Orphanage
        would have done no one any good.
Do you think not?
ALVING. Do you think it would?
It is a terrible misfortune, all the same.

ALVING. Let us speak of it plainly, as a matter of business.—Are
        you waiting for Mr. Manders, Engstrand?
That's just what I'm a-doing of, ma'am.
ALVING. Then sit down meanwhile.
Thank you, ma'am; I'd as soon stand.
ALVING.  I suppose you are going by the steamer?
Yes; it starts in an hour.

ALVING. Then be so good as to take all the papers with you. I won't
        hear another word about this affair. I have other things to think of—
Mrs. Alving—

ALVING. Later on I shall send you a Power of Attorney to settle
        everything as you please.

That I will very readily undertake. The original destination of
        the endowment must now be completely changed, alas!
ALVING. Of course it must.

I think, first of all, I shall arrange that the Solvik property
        shall pass to the parish. The land is by no means without value. It can
        always be turned to account for some purpose or other. And the interest
        of the money in the Bank I could, perhaps, best apply for the benefit of
        some undertaking of acknowledged value to the town.

ALVING. Do just as you please. The whole matter is now completely
        indifferent to me.
Give a thought to my Sailors' Home, your Reverence.

Upon my word, that is not a bad suggestion. That must be
        considered.
Oh, devil take considering—Lord forgive me!

And unfortunately I cannot tell how long I shall
        be able to retain control of these things—whether public opinion
        may not compel me to retire. It entirely depends upon the result of the
        official inquiry into the fire—
ALVING. What are you talking about?
And the result can by no means be foretold.

Ay, but it can though. For here stands
        old Jacob Engstrand.
Well well, but—?

And Jacob Engstrand isn't the man to desert a
        noble benefactor in the hour of need, as the saying goes.
Yes, but my good fellow—how—?

Jacob Engstrand may be likened to a sort of a guardian angel,
        he may, your Reverence.
No, no; I really cannot accept that.

Oh, that'll be the way of it, all the same. I know a man as
        has taken others' sins upon himself before now, I do.

Jacob!  Yours is a rare nature. Well, you
        shall be helped with your Sailors' Home. That you may rely upon.

And now let us
        set out. We two will go together.

You come along
        too, my lass. You shall live as snug as the yolk in an egg.

Merci! [She goes out into the hall and
        fetches MANDERS' overcoat.]

Good-bye, Mrs. Alving! and may the spirit of Law and Order
        descend upon this house, and that quickly.

ALVING. Good-bye, Pastor Manders. [She goes up towards the
        conservatory, as she sees OSWALD coming in through the garden door.]

Good-bye, my child. And if any trouble should come to you, you know
        where Jacob Engstrand is to be found.  Little Harbour Street,
        h'm—!  And the refuge for wandering
        mariners shall be called "Chamberlain Alving's Home," that it shall! And
        if so be as I'm spared to carry on that house in my own way, I make so
        bold as to promise that it shall be worthy of the Chamberlain's memory.

H'm—h'm!—Come along, my dear
        Engstrand. Good-bye! Good-bye! [He and ENGSTRAND go out through the
        hall.]
What house was he talking about?

ALVING. Oh, a kind of Home that he and Pastor Manders want to set
        up.
It will burn down like the other.
ALVING. What makes you think so?

Everything will burn. All that recalls father's memory is
        doomed. Here am I, too, burning down.

ALVING. Oswald! You oughtn't to have remained so long down there,
        my poor boy.
I almost think you are right.

ALVING. Let me dry your face, Oswald; you are quite wet. [She dries
        his face with her pocket-handkerchief.]
Thanks, mother.
ALVING. Are you not tired, Oswald? Should you like to sleep?

No, no—not to sleep! I never sleep. I only
        pretend to.  That will come soon enough.

ALVING.  Yes, you really are ill, my
        blessed boy.
Is Mr. Alving ill?
Oh, do shut all the doors! This killing dread—
ALVING. Close the doors, Regina.

REGINA shuts them and remains standing by the hall door. MRS. ALVING
        takes her shawl off: REGINA does the same. MRS. ALVING draws a chair
        across to OSWALD'S, and sits by him.
ALVING. There now! I am going to sit beside you—

Yes, do. And Regina shall stay here too. Regina shall be with me
        always. You will come to the rescue, Regina, won't you?
I don't understand—
ALVING. To the rescue?
Yes—when the need comes.
ALVING. Oswald, have you not your mother to come to the rescue?

You?  No, mother; that rescue you will never bring me.
         You! ha ha!  Though, after all,
        who ought to do it if not you?  Why can't you say "thou"
        to me, Regina?  Why don't you
        call me "Oswald"?
I don't think Mrs. Alving would like it.

ALVING. You shall have leave to, presently. And meanwhile sit over
        here beside us.

REGINA seats herself demurely and hesitatingly at the other side of the
        table.

ALVING. And now, my poor suffering boy, I am going to take the
        burden off your mind—
You, mother?

ALVING.—all the gnawing remorse and self-reproach you speak
        of.
And you think you can do that?

ALVING. Yes, now I can, Oswald. A little while ago you spoke of the
        joy of life; and at that word a new light burst for me over my life and
        everything connected with it.
I don't understand you.

ALVING. You ought to have known your father when he was a young
        lieutenant. He was brimming over with the joy of life!
Yes, I know he was.

ALVING. It was like a breezy day only to look at him. And what
        exuberant strength and vitality there was in him!
Well—?

ALVING. Well then, child of joy as he was—for he was like a
        child in those days—he had to live at home here in a half-grown
        town, which had no joys to offer him—only dissipations. He had no
        object in life—only an official position. He had no work into
        which he could throw himself heart and soul; he had only business. He
        had not a single comrade that could realise what the joy of life meant—only
        loungers and boon-companions—
Mother—!
ALVING. So the inevitable happened.
The inevitable?

ALVING. You told me yourself, this evening, what would become of
        you if you stayed at home.
Do you mean to say that father—?

ALVING. Your poor father found no outlet for the overpowering joy
        of life that was in him. And I brought no brightness into his home.
Not even you?

ALVING. They had taught me a great deal about duties and so forth,
        which I went on obstinately believing in. Everything was marked out into
        duties—into my duties, and his duties, and—I am afraid I
        made his home intolerable for your poor father, Oswald.
Why have you never spoken of this in writing to me?

ALVING. I have never before seen it in such a light that I could
        speak of it to you, his son.
In what light did you see it, then?

ALVING.  I saw only this one thing: that your father was a
        broken-down man before you were born.
Ah—!

ALVING. And then; day after day, I dwelt on the one thought that by
        rights Regina should be at home in this house—just like my own
        boy.
Regina—!
ALVING. Yes, now you know it, both of you.
Regina!
So mother was that kind of woman.
ALVING. Your mother had many good qualities, Regina.

Yes, but she was one of that sort, all the same. Oh, I've often
        suspected it; but—And now, if you please, ma'am, may I be allowed
        to go away at once?
ALVING. Do you really wish it, Regina?
Yes, indeed I do.
ALVING. Of course you can do as you like; but—
Go away now? Your place is here.

Merci, Mr. Alving!—or now, I suppose, I may say
        Oswald. But I can tell you this wasn't at all what I expected.
ALVING. Regina, I have not been frank with you—

No, that you haven't indeed. If I'd known that Oswald was an
        invalid, why—And now, too, that it can never come to anything
        serious between us—I really can't stop out here in the country and
        wear myself out nursing sick people.
Not even one who is so near to you?

No, that I can't. A poor girl must make the best of her young
        days, or she'll be left out in the cold before she knows where she is.
        And I, too, have the joy of life in me, Mrs. Alving!

ALVING. Unfortunately, you leave. But don't throw yourself away,
        Regina.

Oh, what must be, must be. If Oswald takes after his father, I
        take after my mother, I daresay.—May I ask, ma'am, if Pastor
        Manders knows all this about me?
ALVING. Pastor Manders knows all about it.

Well then, I'd better make
        haste and get away by this steamer. The Pastor is such a nice man to
        deal with; and I certainly think I've as much right to a little of that
        money as he has—that brute of a carpenter.
ALVING. You are heartily welcome to it, Regina.

I think you might have brought me up as a
        gentleman's daughter, ma'am; it would have suited me better. [Tosses her
        head.] But pooh—what does it matter! [With a bitter side glance at
        the corked bottle.] I may come to drink champagne with gentlefolks yet.
ALVING. And if you ever need a home, Regina, come to me.

No, thank you, ma'am. Pastor Manders will look after me, I know.
        And if the worst comes to the worst, I know of one house where I've
        every right to a place.
ALVING. Where is that?
"Chamberlain Alving's Home."
ALVING. Regina—now I see it—you are going to your ruin.
Oh, stuff! Good-bye.
Is she gone?
ALVING. Yes.
I think it was a mistake, this.

ALVING. 
        Oswald, my dear boy—has it shaken you very much?

All that about father, do you
        mean?

ALVING. Yes, about your unhappy father. I am so afraid it may have
        been too much for you.

Why should you fancy that? Of course it came upon me as a great
        surprise; but it can make no real difference to me.

ALVING.  No difference! That your father was
        so infinitely unhappy!
Of course I can pity him, as I would anybody else; but—
ALVING. Nothing more! Your own father!

Oh, "father,"—"father"! I never knew
        anything of father. I remember nothing about him, except that he once
        made me sick.

ALVING. This is terrible to think of! Ought not a son to love his
        father, whatever happens?

When a son has nothing to thank his father for? has never known
        him? Do you really cling to that old superstition?—you who are so
        enlightened in other ways?
ALVING. Can it be only a superstition—?

Yes; surely you can see that, mother. It's one of those notions
        that are current in the world, and so—
ALVING.  Ghosts!
Yes; you may call them ghosts.
ALVING.  Oswald—then you don't love me, either!
You I know, at any rate—
ALVING. Yes, you know me; but is that all!

And, of course, I know how fond you are of me, and I can't but
        be grateful to you. And then you can be so useful to me, now that I am
        ill.

ALVING. Yes, cannot I, Oswald? Oh, I could almost bless the illness
        that has driven you home to me. For I see very plainly that you are not
        mine: I have to win you.

Yes yes yes; all these are just so many phrases.
        You must remember that I am a sick man, mother. I can't be much taken up
        with other people; I have enough to do thinking about myself.
ALVING.  I shall be patient and easily satisfied.
And cheerful too, mother!

ALVING. Yes, my dear boy, you are quite right. 
        Have I relieved you of all remorse and self-reproach now?
Yes, you have. But now who will relieve me of the dread?
ALVING. The dread?
Regina could have been got to do it.

ALVING. I don't understand you. What is this about dread—and
        Regina?
Is it very late, mother?

ALVING. It is early morning. [She looks out through the
        conservatory.] The day is dawning over the mountains. And the weather is
        clearing, Oswald. In a little while you shall see the sun.

I'm glad of that. Oh, I may still have much to rejoice in and
        live for—
ALVING. I should think so, indeed!
Even if I can't work—

ALVING. Oh, you'll soon be able to work again, my dear boy—now
        that you haven't got all those gnawing and depressing thoughts to brood
        over any longer.

Yes, I'm glad you were able to rid me of all those fancies. And
        when I've got over this one thing more— Now we
        will have a little talk, mother—

ALVING. Yes, let us. [She pushes an arm-chair towards the sofa, and
        sits down close to him.]

And meantime the sun will be rising. And then you will know all.
        And then I shall not feel this dread any longer.
ALVING. What is it that I am to know?

Mother, did you not say a little while
        ago, that there was nothing in the world you would not do for me, if I
        asked you?
ALVING. Yes, indeed I said so!
And you'll stick to it, mother?

ALVING. You may rely on that, my dear and only boy! I have nothing
        in the world to live for but you alone.

Very well, then; now you shall hear—Mother, you have a
        strong, steadfast mind, I know. Now you're to sit quite still when you
        hear it.
ALVING. What dreadful thing can it be—?

You're not to scream out. Do you hear? Do you promise me that?
        We will sit and talk about it quietly. Do you promise me, mother?
ALVING. Yes, yes; I promise. Only speak!

Well, you must know that all this fatigue—and my inability
        to think of work—all that is not the illness itself—
ALVING. Then what is the illness itself?

The disease I have as my birthright—[He points to his
        forehead and adds very softly]—is seated here.
ALVING.  Oswald! No—no!

Don't scream. I can't bear it. Yes, mother, it is seated here
        waiting. And it may break out any day—at any moment.
ALVING. Oh, what horror—!
Now, quiet, quiet. That is how it stands with me—

ALVING.  It's not true, Oswald! It's impossible! It
        cannot be so!

I have had one attack down there already. It was soon over. But
        when I came to know the state I had been in, then the dread descended
        upon me, raging and ravening; and so I set off home to you as fast as I
        could.
ALVING. Then this is the dread—!

Yes—it's so indescribably loathsome, you know. Oh, if it
        had only been an ordinary mortal disease—! For I'm not so afraid
        of death—though I should like to live as long as I can.
ALVING. Yes, yes, Oswald, you must!

But this is so unutterably loathsome. To become a little baby
        again! To have to be fed! To have to—Oh, it's not to be spoken of!
ALVING. The child has his mother to nurse him.

No, never that! That is just what I will not have.
        I can't endure to think that perhaps I should lie in that state for many
        years—and get old and grey. And in the meantime you might die and
        leave me.  For the doctor said it wouldn't
        necessarily prove fatal at once. He called it a sort of softening of the
        brain—or something like that.  I think that
        expression sounds so nice. It always sets me thinking of cherry-coloured
        velvet—something soft and delicate to stroke.
ALVING.  Oswald!

And now you have taken Regina
        from me. If I could only have had her! She would have come to the
        rescue, I know.

ALVING.  What do you mean by that, my darling boy? Is
        there any help in the world that I would not give you?

When I got over my attack in Paris, the doctor told me that when
        it comes again—and it will come—there will be no more hope.
ALVING. He was heartless enough to—

I demanded it of him. I told him I had preparations to make—[He
        smiles cunningly.] And so I had. [He takes a little box from his inner
        breast pocket and opens it.] Mother, do you see this?
ALVING. What is it?
Morphia.
ALVING.  Oswald—my boy!
I've scraped together twelve pilules—
ALVING.  Give me the box, Oswald.
Not yet, mother.
ALVING. I shall never survive this!

It must be survived. Now if I'd had Regina here, I should have
        told her how things stood with me—and begged her to come to the
        rescue at the last. She would have done it. I know she would.
ALVING. Never!

When the horror had come upon me, and she saw me lying there
        helpless, like a little new-born baby, impotent, lost, hopeless—past
        all saving—
ALVING. Never in all the world would Regina have done this!

Regina would have done it. Regina was so splendidly
        light-hearted. And she would soon have wearied of nursing an invalid
        like me.
ALVING. Then heaven be praised that Regina is not here.
Well then, it is you that must come to the rescue, mother.
ALVING.  I!
Who should do it if not you?
ALVING. I! your mother!
For that very reason.
ALVING. I, who gave you life!

I never asked you for life. And what sort of a life have you
        given me? I will not have it! You shall take it back again!
ALVING. Help! Help!
Do not leave me! Where are you going?
ALVING.  To fetch the doctor, Oswald! Let me pass!
You shall not go out. And no one shall come in.
ALVING.  Oswald! Oswald—my child!

Have you a mother's heart for me—and yet
        can see me suffer from this unutterable dread?

ALVING. 
        Here is my hand upon it.
Will you—?

ALVING. If it should ever be necessary. But it will never be
        necessary. No, no; it is impossible.

Well, let us hope so. And let us live together as long as we
        can. Thank you, mother. [He seats himself in the arm-chair which MRS.
        ALVING has moved to the sofa. Day is breaking. The lamp is still burning
        on the table.]
ALVING.  Do you feel calm now?
Yes.

ALVING.  It has been a dreadful fancy of yours,
        Oswald—nothing but a fancy. All this excitement has been too much
        for you. But now you shall have a long rest; at home with your mother, my
        own blessëd boy. Everything you point to you shall have, just as when
        you were a little child.—There now. The crisis is over. You see
        how easily it passed! Oh, I was sure it would.—And do you see,
        Oswald, what a lovely day we are going to have? Brilliant sunshine! Now
        you can really see your home. [She goes to the table and puts out the
        lamp. Sunrise. The glacier and the snow-peaks in the background glow in
        the morning light.]

[Sits in the arm-chair with his back towards the landscape,
        without moving. Suddenly he says:] Mother, give me the sun.
ALVING.  What do you say?
The sun. The sun.
ALVING.  Oswald, what is the matter with you?

[Seems to shrink together to the chair; all his muscles relax;
        his face is expressionless, his eyes have a glassy stare.]

ALVING.  What is this?  Oswald!
        what is the matter with you? [Falls on her knees beside him and shakes
        him.] Oswald! Oswald! look at me! Don't you know me?
The sun.—The sun.

ALVING. [Springs up in despair, entwines her hands in her hair and
        shrieks.] I cannot bear it! ; I cannot
        bear it! Never!  Where has he got them? [Fumbles hastily in
        his breast.] Here!  No! No; no!—Yes!—No;
        no!

She stands a few steps away from him with her hands twisted in her
        hair, and stares at him in speechless horror.
The sun.—The sun.
